    OS Compatibility Audit

    02_os-compatibility.Rmd

    Overview

    utilrep2 includes a set of auditing functions to help identify Windows compatibility issues in a correlates_reporting2 repository before attempting to run the pipeline on Windows or WSL.

    The audit covers two dimensions:

    1. Code patterns — active (non-commented) R/Rmd lines that use Unix-only idioms (e.g. mclapply(), doMC, system("export ..."))
    2. renv packages — locked packages that cannot be installed on Windows or require Rtools to compile from source

    Full audit in one call

    check_os_compatibility() runs both checks and returns a structured os_check_result object. Calling print() on it produces a formatted console report:

    library(utilrep2)

result <- check_os_compatibility(root = here::here())
print(result)
    ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  OS Compatibility Report
----------------------------------------------------------------------

  Total issues : 3
  Critical     : 2
  Moderate     : 1
  Low          : 0

CODE PATTERNS
----------------------------------------------------------------------

  [CRITICAL] mclapply() silently falls back to single-threaded on Windows
  Fix     : Replace with future.apply::future_lapply() + plan(multisession)
  Files   :
    - cor_surrogates/code/run_cvsl_varsets.R
    ...

    The result object has three elements:

    names(result)
# [1] "code_patterns"  "packages"  "summary"

    result$summary is a flat data frame with one row per issue — convenient for filtering or exporting to a spreadsheet:

    result$summary[result$summary$severity == "critical", c("category", "item", "fix")]

    Scanning code patterns only

    check_code_patterns() recursively searches all .R and .Rmd files for Unix-only idioms. The default exclusion list skips renv/, tests/, win/, and os_check/:

    hits <- check_code_patterns(root = here::here())
hits

    The returned data frame has columns file, id, pattern, description, severity, and fix.

    Customising the exclusion list 

    hits <- check_code_patterns(
  root         = here::here(),
  exclude_dirs = c("renv", "tests", "win", "archive")
)

    You can also pass exclude_dirs directly through check_os_compatibility() via ..., without calling check_code_patterns() separately:

    result <- check_os_compatibility(
  root         = here::here(),
  exclude_dirs = c("renv", "tests", "win", "archive")
)

    Customising the pattern catalogue

    unix_patterns() returns the built-in catalogue as a data frame with columns id, pattern, description, severity, and fix. You can extend or replace it:

    # View the built-in catalogue
unix_patterns()

# Add a custom pattern
extra <- data.frame(
  id          = "rawconnection",
  pattern     = "rawConnection\\(",
  description = "rawConnection() behaves differently on Windows",
  severity    = "moderate",
  fix         = "Use tempfile() + writeBin() instead",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

my_patterns <- rbind(unix_patterns(), extra)

# Pass directly to check_code_patterns()
check_code_patterns(root = here::here(), patterns = my_patterns)

# Or pass through the wrapper via ...
check_os_compatibility(root = here::here(), patterns = my_patterns)

    Checking renv.lock packages

    check_renv_packages() parses renv.lock and queries CRAN for three potential problems:

    Check Severity Meaning
    OS_type: unix critical Package will not install on Windows at all
    No Windows binary moderate Package can be installed but requires Rtools
    GitHub + NeedsCompilation: yes low Requires Rtools on Windows 
    pkg_issues <- check_renv_packages(
  lock_path   = here::here("renv.lock"),
  cran_mirror = "https://cran.rstudio.com/"
)

pkg_issues$unix_only          # packages that cannot install on Windows
pkg_issues$no_win_binary      # packages that require Rtools
pkg_issues$github_needs_compile  # GitHub packages needing compilation

    Network requirement: Checks 1–3 all require internet access to query the CRAN database. The function degrades gracefully if the network is unavailable, emitting a message and returning empty data frames.

    Skipping the package check

    Pass check_packages = FALSE to check_os_compatibility() to run only the source-code scan (no network required):

    result <- check_os_compatibility(
  root           = here::here(),
  check_packages = FALSE
)

    Custom renv.lock location

    By default check_os_compatibility() looks for renv.lock inside root. If the lockfile is elsewhere, pass lock_path explicitly:

    result <- check_os_compatibility(
  root      = here::here(),
  lock_path = "/shared/project/renv.lock"
)

    Interpreting severity levels

    Severity Meaning
    critical Will fail or produce wrong results on Windows without a fix
    moderate Will likely fail without extra tooling (e.g. Rtools), or may silently degrade
    low Needs attention but has a straightforward workaround

    Suggested workflow

    1. Run the full audit once before porting to Windows:

      result <- check_os_compatibility(root = here::here())
print(result)

    2. Export critical issues for tracking:

      critical <- result$summary[result$summary$severity == "critical", ]
write.csv(critical, "critical_issues.csv", row.names = FALSE)

    3. After applying fixes, re-run to confirm the issue count drops to zero.

    Developed by Y H.

    Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
